channel.title: American Enterprise Institute – AEI
channel.description: The American Enterprise Institute, AEI, is a nonpartisan public policy research institute with a community of scholars and supporters committed to expanding liberty, increasing individual opportunity and strengthening free enterprise.

item.title: When Will AI Affect US Productivity Growth?
item.dc:creator: Ellison Winger
item.description:
Even if we’re still in the beginning of the beginning of an AI-driven economic revolution, is it still so early that we’re likely not yet seeing a broad impact on labor productivity and economic growth?

The post When Will AI Affect US Productivity Growth? appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
By now, it’s pretty clear that generative AI can do lots of important business tasks. It can write code, summarize research, design marketing campaigns, and automate customer service. 

Take the case of Walmart. The retail giant has partnered with OpenAI and Google to make its products easier to discover and buy, while building its own AI shopping assistant, Sparky. At its recent earnings call, CEO John Furner said half of its app users have already tried the smiley face helper. Customers who use Sparky spend more per order on average. That’s a notable real-world data point for all the remaining AI skeptics out there, although the company acknowledges it is still in the early days of figuring out how AI will affect its business long-term.

Some economists are really encouraged by what they see taking place. In a recent Financial Times op-ed, Stanford’s Erik Brynjolfsson argues that the broader data may already be hinting at an important inflection point in the emerging age of AI. His confidence rests on a variety of observations: rapid advances in AI capability, a growing body of micro studies showing double-digit gains in specific tasks, evidence of employment shifts in AI-exposed occupations, and reports from “power users” and executives who say workflows are beginning to change. 

From the piece:

My own updated analysis suggests a US productivity increase of roughly 2.7 per cent for 2025. This is a near doubling from the sluggish 1.4 per cent annual average that characterised the past decade. This shift aligns with the productivity “J-curve” that my colleagues and I have explored in earlier research. General-purpose technologies, from the steam engine to the computer, do not deliver immediate gains. Instead, they require a period of massive, often unmeasured investment in intangible capital — reorganising business processes, retraining the workforce and developing new business models. During this phase, measured productivity is suppressed as resources are diverted to investments. The updated 2025 US data suggests we are now transitioning out of this investment phase into a harvest phase where those earlier efforts begin to manifest as measurable output.

But even if we’re still in the beginning of the beginning of an AI-driven economic revolution, is it still so early that we’re likely not yet seeing a broad impact on labor productivity and economic growth?

There’s certainly reason for caution. As economist Martha Gimbel at Yale’s Budget Lab notes, productivity data are notoriously noisy. When GDP keeps rising while job growth slows, as has been happening lately in the US economy, it can look like workers are suddenly producing more. But there could be other explanations, as Gimbel explains. During the pandemic, millions of lower-wage service workers lost jobs, which mechanically raised average output per worker even though no one became more efficient. More recently, immigration policy changes have reduced labor-force growth. If workers leaving the labor force tend to be in lower-productivity roles, measured productivity can rise without any AI breakthrough. One or two quarters are hardly enough to prove a lasting shift.

The recent NBER Working Paper “Firm Data on AI” might help square the circle here. It finds that 70 percent of firms report using AI, yet 90 percent say it has had no measurable impact on productivity or employment so far, and senior executives themselves use it only about 1.5 hours per week, although “this frequency has increased sharply since the start of 2025.” So for now, adoption appears widespread but still rather shallow.

What’s more, in the survey of nearly 6,000 senior executives across four advanced economies, adoption is concentrated in larger, more productive firms, whose leaders expect AI to raise productivity by 1.4 percent over the next three years. That pattern seems consistent with the idea that early “harvest” effects—again, where firms begin to see measurable gains after investing in AI and reorganizing workflows—may be jaggedly emerging at the frontier, even as economy-wide transformation remains incomplete.

Again, not the end of the beginning but still the beginning of the beginning. Let the progress continue.

The post When Will AI Affect US Productivity Growth? appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: A Standard Worth Inheriting
item.dc:creator: Ellison Winger
item.description:
That is how legacy works. It becomes instinct. Formation does not wait for adulthood; it begins the moment we learn whom to admire. A century like hers does not simply end. It sets a standard worth inheriting.

The post A Standard Worth Inheriting appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
When her Baubie—Sylvia Wagner—recently died at 100, my daughter, Natalie, cried in a way that told me she understood something real had changed. She could not articulate it. But she recognized absence. She recognized weight. At four, you do not measure a century in historical eras. You measure it in presence: who sits at the table, who listens carefully, who feels steady when they enter a room. That is what she lost.

But Sylvia Wagner was more than a beloved great-grandmother. Born in Omaha to Jewish immigrants from Latvia and Belorussia, she came of age in a household that treated hard work, education, and civic responsibility as obligations, not aspirations. That formation held. She went on to serve on the Omaha City Council representing the Sixth District, one of the few women in municipal leadership at the time, and she approached that role with intellectual seriousness and moral courage.

Her tenure was not ceremonial. She championed Nebraska’s first group homes for adults with mental disabilities—standing firm against public fear and organized resistance—and worked to open Omaha’s streets to outdoor dining when that required confronting bureaucratic inertia as much as public skepticism. When the recall of Mayor Boyle became a political flashpoint, she opposed it, not because it was popular but because she believed constructive governance mattered more than political theater. She treated public office as a trust. Preparation mattered. Words carried consequence. Disagreement required argument, not theatrics.

Her colleagues respected her Sabbath observance. That detail matters. For Sylvia Wagner, Jewish identity was not a background fact. It was the framework through which she understood obligation; to family, to community, to the city she served. The same tradition that shaped her private life informed her public ethics. She was one person all the way through.

A hundred years does not merely accumulate birthdays. It tests a person. It reveals what bends with fashion and what endures. What Sylvia Wagner revealed over a century was disciplined leadership—strength anchored in study, conviction tempered by humility. She was not quiet. She had strong views and did not hesitate to voice them. But her confidence was rooted in preparation, not impulse; in duty, not display. Her voice carried weight because it was earned.

I think of one Passover not long ago. Natalie was small enough to climb easily into her Baubie’s lap. The Haggadah lay open between them. Natalie traced the Hebrew letters with her finger, asking questions in the way only a child can—curious, insistent, delighted by the ritual. Sylvia leaned in, following the text closely, guiding her through the story of freedom and responsibility. At that end of the table, learning was serious. Questions were welcomed. Tradition was not recited mechanically but engaged. That is formation.

Across her century, Sylvia Wagner lived through wars, economic hardship, political turbulence, and cultural change. She neither retreated into cynicism nor mistook noise for influence. She strengthened what she touched; her family, her community, her city.

There is a Jewish phrase we say after someone dies: may her memory be a blessing. It is not sentimental. It is a charge. It asks whether we will allow the life we witnessed to shape our own. Sylvia Wagner’s century is a blessing not because it was long, but because it was serious; because it showed that a woman can be intellectually rigorous, morally courageous, publicly engaged, and deeply devoted to family, all at once, without apology.

Natalie was fortunate to have sat on her lap, to have traced experience Jewish traditions under her guidance, to have absorbed that steadiness before she could name it. Not every girl grows up seeing female authority joined to humility, conviction joined to preparation, leadership joined to love. All girls should be so lucky. A society that wants strong institutions must form strong women and that formation begins early: at tables, in laps, in the quiet modeling of adulthood lived seriously.

She cannot yet articulate what she lost. But as she ages, I hope she will understand what she received: intellectual seriousness without arrogance, moral courage without cruelty, leadership without vanity, love without condition.

One day, she will sit at her own table. She will hold her own books. She will form her own arguments. She will lead in ways I cannot yet imagine. When that time comes, I hope there will be something of Sylvia Wagner in her—seriousness without severity, conviction without cruelty, strength without spectacle. Not because she is imitating her great-grandmother, but because she has absorbed the standard she witnessed.

That is how legacy works. It becomes instinct. Formation does not wait for adulthood; it begins the moment we learn whom to admire.

A century like hers does not simply end. It sets a standard worth inheriting.

The post A Standard Worth Inheriting appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: Lunar New Year and the Civic Gift of Belonging
item.dc:creator: Ellison Winger
item.description:
In a time when inclusion is too often bureaucratized, Lunar New Year reminded us that the most durable belonging is still built the old-fashioned way: in shared streets and rituals and simple acts of welcome.

The post Lunar New Year and the Civic Gift of Belonging appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
This week, I took my son downtown for Lunar New Year.

Chinatown was packed shoulder to shoulder. Lanterns hung overhead. Confetti covered the pavement. Storefronts spilled warmth and light into the cold air. Children leaned forward, wide-eyed, as drums echoed down the block.

Then the dragons arrived—towering, roaring, dancing, absurdly alive. Their painted, blinking eyes and snapping mouths moved inches from the curb, bending toward the crowd as if the city itself were offering a blessing. My son, in his bright blue coat, edged right to the front. Strangers made room without hesitation. Adults smiled. Children stared and cheered in shared delight.

For a moment, New York felt less like a machine and more like a neighborhood. And what stayed with me from the festival was not only the spectacle, but the welcome.

We were included—not as outsiders performing appreciation, not as spectators politely observing someone else’s celebration, but as neighbors drawn into a public ritual of renewal. We learned. We laughed. We appreciated. We celebrated together.

This is how multiculturalism and real inclusion actually works.

Not through mandated language or institutional choreography. Not through bureaucratic frameworks that professionalize identity and turn belonging into an administrative project. But through lived encounter: communities confident enough in their traditions to open them outward, and citizens willing to receive that invitation with gratitude.

Too much of modern diversity discourse has become abstract, adversarial, or grievance-soaked. It is filtered through institutions that reduce human difference to categories and treat civic life as a negotiation among competing blocs. The result is often a politics of separation rather than a culture of encounter.

But Lunar New Year in Chinatown offers something different. It is pluralism made tangible: a public celebration rooted in particular histories and inheritances, yet generously shared with the broader city. No one handed us a pamphlet. No one asked us to rehearse the language of inclusion. There were no seminars about behavior and norms. There was simply belonging.

And that matters because belonging cannot be conjured by administrative fiat. It is not the product of a training module or a strategic plan. It emerges from the kinds of shared practices that build trust over time—what Robert Putnam famously described as the everyday social capital that makes democratic life possible.

We spend enormous energy today debating diversity at the level of institutions: policies, offices, statements, metrics. But civic cohesion is often forged elsewhere, in the ordinary rituals of neighborhood life, where people encounter one another not as abstractions or demographic symbols, but as fellow citizens and parents and children standing on the same curb.

What I saw that afternoon was not a lesson in politics, but in civic formation. My son was not thinking about “multiculturalism” as an ideology. He was simply experiencing the city as a shared home with others—one in which traditions are not hidden away, but offered publicly, with generosity.

This is the kind of experience that builds civic trust far more effectively than any institutional statement. A healthy democracy depends on more of these ordinary, embodied moments where difference becomes familiarity and strangers become neighbors.

That matters, especially now.

In an age of loneliness, fragmentation, and institutional mistrust, thick communal rituals are not ornamental. They are civic infrastructure. They teach continuity. Obligation. Reverence for those who came before. Hope for those who come next.

Lunar New Year is not merely aesthetic. The parades, the family meals, the symbolism of luck and renewal: these are forms of moral education. They remind us that human beings are not self-made. We are shaped by inheritance, community, memory, and place.

Watching my son take it all in, I was struck by how rare such unmediated public joy has become. So much of childhood today is individualized and curated, mediated through screens and algorithmic attention. But here was something embodied and communal. He wasn’t consuming content. He was participating in a city.

And the city, briefly, rose to the occasion.

People made space for one another’s children. The atmosphere was not performative or tense. Difference was not weaponized. It was offered.

This is the quiet genius of American pluralism when it works: not the flattening of distinct traditions, but the sharing of them. Not a politics of perpetual accusation, but a culture of invitation.

It depends on rituals that gather us, traditions that endure, and public spaces where strangers can become neighbors, even briefly.

In an era when inclusion is too often treated as an administrative slogan, a dragon dance on a narrow street downtown offered something far more persuasive: inclusion practiced.

In a time when inclusion is too often bureaucratized, Lunar New Year reminded us that the most durable belonging is still built the old-fashioned way: in shared streets and rituals and simple acts of welcome.

The post Lunar New Year and the Civic Gift of Belonging appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: Felicia Was There
item.dc:creator: Ellison Winger
item.description:
Some gifts cannot be repaid. Many families have known someone like Felicia. The question worth sitting with is whether we appreciate that work for what it truly is: not a service transaction, but the connective tissue of family life itself.

The post Felicia Was There appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
Sylvia Wagner lived to 100.

A full century. Children, grandchildren, and great-grandchildren. Holidays gathered around family tables. Stories told and retold. The long unfolding of a family across generations.

She lived a good and generous life.

But the final decade was not easy.

Longevity is often described as a triumph of modern medicine and prosperity. And it is. But what we speak of less often is what those added years require. Aging is not tidy. It is slow. It can be disorienting. Independence narrows. Memory falters. The body does not always cooperate.

In those years, what sustains dignity is not technology. It is presence.

Felicia was there.

Not occasionally. Not ceremonially. But steadily. Faithfully. For years, through the long arc of Sylvia’s final decade. In the ordinary, repetitive, unseen work that fills the days of advanced age. She helped Sylvia rise in the morning and settle at night. She prepared meals. She kept track of medications. She navigated frustration with patience. She offered reassurance when the world felt unfamiliar.

But Felicia did more than manage decline.

She enabled Sylvia to travel—to leave her hometown of Omaha and come east to see her children and grandchildren and great-grandchildren. She made it possible for Sylvia to sit at Passover Seders, in Virginia Beach to hear the youngest ask the Four Questions, to feel the rhythm of family ritual. She ensured that Sylvia could enjoy a proper New York meal and see a Broadway show, dressed up and out in the world, rather than confined by frailty.

She preserved not only comfort, but participation.

Felicia also carried responsibilities. Her son serves in the United States military—which means she understands sacrifice not as an abstraction but as something she lives with daily, even as she showed up steadily in someone else’s. And yet she remained faithful. She showed up, day after day, with grace.

I remember watching Sylvia seated at the table, alert and engaged, alive in the conversation—and I knew that it had taken careful, quiet effort to make that moment possible. Medications packed. Logistics arranged. Energy managed. Patience before and after the celebration.

Felicia made those moments happen.

Caregiving at the end of life is intimate work. It is physical and emotional. It requires attentiveness not only to the body but to the spirit. To know when to encourage, when to rest, when to insist gently, and when simply to sit. To understand that capability is not merely about mobility, but about belonging.

Because of Felicia, my family could be children and grandchildren and great-grandchildren—not full-time nurses. We could listen to Sylvia’s stories rather than constantly calculate risk. We could celebrate her hundredth birthday with gratitude rather than anxiety. We could love her as family, trusting that someone steady was tending to the daily details.

That is no small gift. And it points to something that policy discussions about elder care so often miss: the human reality behind the abstractions. We debate funding mechanisms and regulatory frameworks, but what sustains dignity in old age is a person who shows up, who pays attention, who makes participation possible.

Caregiving rarely commands public recognition. It unfolds behind closed doors. It is repetitive. It can be exhausting. And yet it may be among the most consequential work done in any family. It is not only about preserving life. It is about preserving dignity within life.

Felicia understood that.

She did not extend Sylvia’s years; no one commands time. But she made those years more humane, more connected, more participatory than they might otherwise have been. She ensured that even in frailty, Sylvia remained part of the living current of her family. She made joy possible.

Gratitude can feel inadequate in proportion to what we owe. I thanked her more than once, but words rarely seem equal to work like this. Some debts are not financial or formal; they are human. They live in memory. They live in the quiet knowledge that someone stood beside your loved one when you could not always be there yourself.

Sylvia’s century is now complete. The stories remain. The generations continue. Great-grandchildren will grow up hearing about her long life and presence.

And I will remember another steady presence too. Felicia was there.

In the quiet mornings. In the long afternoons. In the travel that allowed one more holiday together. In the small acts that made participation possible. In the daily work that protected dignity.

Some gifts cannot be repaid. They can only be honored. Many families have known someone like Felicia—or have needed someone like her. The question worth sitting with is whether we appreciate that work for what it truly is: not a service transaction, but the connective tissue of family life itself.

This is my attempt to do just that.

The post Felicia Was There appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: Conservatives Should Cheer Spanberger’s Williamsburg Speech
item.dc:creator: Ellison Winger
item.description:
A free society does not require a flawless origin story. It requires citizens confident enough to argue about the nation’s direction while honoring a shared inheritance that was always unfinished. Williamsburg is where that work began.

The post Conservatives Should Cheer Spanberger’s Williamsburg Speech appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
On Tuesday evening, Virginia Governor Abigail Spanberger will deliver the Democratic response to the State of the Union from Colonial Williamsburg. Some conservatives are already sharpening their knives. They should reconsider.

The instinct of many conservative critics is understandable. Spanberger has clashed with the Trump administration on immigration, federal workforce policy, and redistricting. Her use of one of America’s most storied settings as a backdrop for a partisan rebuttal will strike some on the right as lending hallowed ground to the opposition. Cliff Fleet, Colonial Williamsburg’s president and CEO, anticipated this—his letter to supporters noted that the Foundation “has welcomed US presidents from both political parties throughout its history” and has “always welcomed Virginia’s governors.”

But critics are getting this exactly backward.

Start with the history. The Williamsburg Capitol is not merely a picturesque backdrop of brick façades and tricorn hats. The chamber where Spanberger will stand is the same room where, on May 15, 1776, delegates to the Fifth Virginia Convention unanimously instructed their representatives in Philadelphia “to declare the United Colonies free and independent states.” That resolution, carried to Congress by Richard Henry Lee, became the motion that produced the Declaration of Independence. Virginia had a functioning republican constitution and an inaugurated governor—Patrick Henry—before Congress declared independence on July 4. The Revolution did not begin in Philadelphia. It began in Williamsburg.

We are less than three months from the 250th anniversary of that May 15 resolution—one of the most consequential acts of political courage in modern history. That Colonial Williamsburg will be in the national spotlight, that Americans will hear its name and perhaps look up what happened there, is an unalloyed good. Every citizen who rediscovers that history is a small victory for civic education at a moment when we badly need it.

I know this place well. During the earliest months of the pandemic, when schools were closed and playgrounds wrapped in caution tape, I began walking Colonial Williamsburg with my son. Those mornings were not nostalgic. They were steadying. The place reminded me that earlier generations lived through crises they did not choose and still built institutions meant to outlast them. Williamsburg gives form to the idea that civic life is not just something we feel. It is something we practice in shared space and I believe that this idea deeply implanted in my son.

That is precisely what makes it the right setting for a political address—even one delivered by a governor whose policies many conservatives oppose. The deeper lesson of Williamsburg is not agreement. It is argument: vigorous, sometimes bitter, yet bounded by shared constitutional commitments. The chamber where Jefferson, Henry, Washington, and Mason forged their vision was not a place of consensus. It was a place of contest. That is what made it great.

Conservatives have spent years arguing that the founding principles are universal, not a partisan relic but a shared inheritance. We have pushed back against those on the left who reduce the founding to little more than hypocrisy. But you cannot credibly make that case and then object when a Democratic governor invokes Williamsburg. If the founding belongs to everyone, then Williamsburg belongs to everyone—including people with whom we disagree.

There is a strategic error here as well. When conservatives protest every Democratic engagement with American history, they cede the narrative. They risk turning Williamsburg into a partisan symbol rather than a common inheritance. They reinforce the caricature that the right treats the founders as mascots rather than statesmen whose ideas demand serious engagement from every generation. If conservatives believe the founding principles are durable, they should have enough confidence to let opponents invoke them.

Alexis de Tocqueville observed that America’s genius lay in its habits of association; in citizens who gathered, argued, and governed themselves. Williamsburg embodies that tradition: a reminder that republican self-government depends not on silence but on speech, not on unanimity but on contest.

Spanberger will stand where the promise of American self-government was first articulated and offer her vision for the country. Conservatives will disagree with much of what she says—vigorously, publicly, and in the tradition of the sacred place itself. The genius of Williamsburg is not harmony but argument: the conviction that serious disagreement, conducted in the open, strengthens rather than weakens the republic.

A free society does not require a flawless origin story. It requires citizens confident enough to argue about the nation’s direction while honoring a shared inheritance that was always unfinished. Williamsburg is where that work began. It is right that it continues there, especially when the speaker is someone with whom many legitimately disagree.

The past is not a museum piece. It is a charge. And it belongs to all of us.

The post Conservatives Should Cheer Spanberger’s Williamsburg Speech appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: Some Clarifications Regarding the New Trade Situation
item.dc:creator: Ellison Winger
item.description:
On Friday, the Supreme Court struck down Trump’s use of emergency authority for many tariffs. Trump showed quick inconsistency, announcing a 10 percent global tariff then raising it a day later. This year will again be tumultuous, but some misperceptions can be dispelled.

The post Some Clarifications Regarding the New Trade Situation appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
Last Thursday, 2025 trade data were published. On Friday, the Supreme Court struck down President Trump’s use of emergency authority for many tariffs. The Court left other matters open, for example rebates. And President Trump showed quickly that consistency is still unwanted, announcing a 10 percent global tariff then raising it to 15 percent a day later. This year will again be tumultuous, but some misperceptions can be dispelled.

Misperception 1:  The trade deficit in goods, which set a record in 2025, will rise further without tariffs imposed under the International Economic Emergency Powers Act (IEEPA).

Clarification: Demand for imports is first determined by our own economy. The better we’re doing, the more imports we want. In terms of policy, uncertainty prevails to start this year and will probably persist. The details of what’s uncertain have changed but the negative impact on imports hasn’t. The value of goods imports may not shift much.

Misperception 2: Tariffs were high and now might plunge.

Clarification: Tariffs weren’t as high as some headlines suggest, can’t fall much, and may not fall at all.

Tariff revenue was equivalent to about 8.5 percent of goods import value last year. It was equivalent to about 12 percent in the fourth quarter alone (this helps explain why tariffs haven’t caused notable inflation). The main difference between the revenue burden on importers and much higher rates often cited was a hefty set of exemptions. These were largely confirmed in the annexes attached to Friday’s global tariff announcement. To preserve effective tariffs, the Trump administration can either trim exemptions or apply new tariffs using other legal authorities.

Misperception 3: IEEPA tariffs can be quickly replaced through Sections 122 and 301 of the 1974 Trade Act, Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, and other tools.

Clarification: They can, but very well may not. Trade investigations can’t always be simple and dramatic, but President Trump doesn’t like announcing small or complex trade actions. The Department of Commerce is responsible for national security inquiries and faces internal challenges ranging from unresolved cases to departing personnel, mostly connected to China policy. National security restrictions that don’t feature China would surely face more challenges.

Another complicating factor is the administration has reasons to stay close to country and product tariff levels cited in negotiations with our partners. Extensive deviations could undermine existing agreements or provoke retaliation. Yet new trade actions that look remarkably similar to IEEPA tariffs, which were themselves announced with sometimes ridiculous justifications, would invite more lawsuits.

Misperception 4: If IEEPA tariffs are refunded, it would be a sizable economic boost. Conversely, the short-term loss to the Treasury would be serious.

Clarification: IEEPA tariffs and potential rebates certainly matter to the companies involved, but don’t matter much to the US as a whole. The total amount is near $140 billion, where rebates would likely be spread out by the legal process. The 2025 tax reductions are estimated to average $450 billion annually for ten years. Annual GDP was $31 trillion last year. The projected federal deficit for fiscal 2026 is $1.9 trillion. Adding $140 billion wouldn’t help but is basically a forecast error. The President and critics treat existing tariffs as vital. They’ve been imposed capriciously, sometimes foolishly; they aren’t vital.

Misperception 5: Bilateral trade agreements stuck by the Trump administration to date are in jeopardy due to the President’s loss of authority.

Clarification: These agreements have close to the same status as before. None were actual treaties ratified by Congress, requiring equivalent steps in partner countries. All were entirely subject to the President’s dissatisfaction with the results or even his personal treatment. Negotiating partners ignoring concessions they made to the US over the past year face a major risk. The US can’t respond immediately if most partners abandon the deals, but President Trump isn’t just going to forget about trade.

Misperception 6: The Court decision weakens the US position versus China.

Clarification: The US position versus China has been weakened far more by the Trump administration. China is by a large margin the easiest country to act against under US trade law—both economic predator and military threat. The Section 301 and 232 findings practically write themselves.

President Trump is the problem. In 2020, as China refused any responsibility for Covid, he took no trade action at all. Since his October 2025 meeting with Xi Jinping, his administration has frozen or reversed China action all on its own, apparently because the President thinks his forthcoming trip to Beijing will be magical. What’s more likely is he will accomplish little, again, and the Court decision will have nothing to do with that.  

The post Some Clarifications Regarding the New Trade Situation appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: Will Drones Become Part of Critical Infrastructure?
item.dc:creator: Julia Torres
item.description:
Shane Tews is joined by Brian Hinman and John Donovan to discuss how a next phase in drone technology could change the future of aviation.

The post Will Drones Become Part of Critical Infrastructure? appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
Drones have previously been confined due to limitations such as short battery lives and restricted load capacities. This is beginning to change. With SiFly recently breaking the world record with an electric drone completing a flight lasting more than three hours, we are seeing drones advance into a new phase of aviation that will be used as a crucial part of critical infrastructure. Why does going from minutes to hours completely change what drones can do in the real world? And how is SiFly building toward an America First manufactured drone that is fundamentally a more sophisticated technology than what already exists?

Here are highlights from my conversation with Brian Hinman, the founder and CEO of SiFly and an adviser at Metrika. I was also joined by John Donovan, the CEO of Qudit Investments and former CEO of AT&T Communications. With my guests’ extensive experience across aviation, cybersecurity, and technology, this discussion goes deep into understanding how this next phase in drones enhances deploying new tools for first responders and how it could change conversations around the future of aviation.

Below is a lightly edited and abridged transcript of our discussion. You can listen to this and other episodes of Explain to Shane on AEI.org and subscribe via your preferred listening platform. If you enjoyed this episode, leave us a review, and tell your friends and colleagues to tune in.

Shane Tews: Let’s start with how you two met and started working together, because you have an interesting story.

Brian Hinman: We go back 20 years, actually. John was my customer when he was at AT&T—he was on my board. We met when I was building DSL equipment and reconnected about two years ago. It’s been fantastic to get back together again.

John Donovan: It’s fortuitous, Shane, that my daughter had an interest in agriculture—regenerative agriculture. I was at the University of California, Davis, and we were out in the field. They were telling us about all this advanced work they were doing with sensing, and the problem was that these darn drones cost so much money and only hang in the air for 20 minutes. I was literally up at UC Davis and I get a call from Brian, so I drove back to Brian’s office to visit him. You talk about serendipity. I already knew the use case in agriculture walking in his front door, and he said, ‘I have this high-endurance platform.’ I said, ‘Perfect.’ I just witnessed how not having high endurance makes it really hard to innovate with devices that only stay in the air for 20 minutes.

I just have to point out, in my 40-year career, I’ve never met a better engineer than Brian. When you get down to the physics of things, he won’t tell you this story but we were in a board meeting once and I had looked and he had opened his notebook and was writing some calculations down. I asked, ‘What are you doing?’ He said, ‘This idea popped in my head on something and I had to write it down.’ I looked at his engineering notebook and it dawned on me: there are people who know technology—they know how to talk about it and how it works. Then there are people who live inside the information box. That’s Brian. That’s Brian in the physical sense, and he has an obsession with perfection at every component in every element every time in anything he’s ever done. And that’s what I love best about him.

Let’s talk about SiFly—what have you created here?

Brian Hinman: Well, just at the beginning of the company, what got me started was thinking about—you want to make something that can fly a long time, that’s electrically powered because you get all the reliability that comes with that. But it kind of requires rethinking how drones are made because as Part 107, it’s all visual line of sight flying under FAA rules. And I’d see the future as flying way beyond visual line of sight—cellular connected, cloud connected.

And what does that look like? It means that the aircraft themselves need to be redesigned from the scratch. And if you look at all the drones that are out there today, they all look like flying bricks and they don’t need to be particularly aerodynamic because they’re typically hovering and are not in forward flight. In essence, what I set out to do four years ago was to create the quadcopter optimized for forward flight. And as it just as an aircraft category. And I think what I’ve ended up with is something that not only works well at the scale that we’re at, but it scales up to much larger sizes.

You have two models that you have been working on. Tell us first about the Q12.

Brian Hinman: Right, it’s actually 12 is for 12 kilograms, that’s how much the thing weighs so the Q12 is really a sensing platform that’s meant to be a drone as first responder. You send it out to the scene, it’s got a high-quality camera on board; other sensors payloads can be added in—thermal cameras, we do multi-spectral mapping for things like agriculture. And again, we flown that thing for over three hours.

We think we—actually at this point—could do over four hours, and very soon we’re going to be able to demonstrate that we can break the world record as far as for electrically powered vertical takeoff and landing aircraft—set the distance record as well. I think for a lot of those missions that require overlying a site, surveillance, you name it, that’s what the Q12 platform is for. The other one is the larger one, the Q250, and that is designed to be carrying payload, serious payload. The initial application we were looking at in agriculture is for spraying and agricultural spraying is something that everybody needs to do and we think we have a platform there that’s going to be the most cost-effective way of doing it and, again, way safer than, for example, using helicopters to do it.

What are the next steps for you all before you can go to market with these amazing things?

Brian Hinman: We’re in the back-end phase of the engineering now. The timing of the change in rules was actually good for us because we’re at the stage of choosing some of our contract manufacturers. And with the emphasis of Made in America, we’re looking at contract manufacturers in Arizona and Texas that would do this subsystem assembly. We’re to do the final assembly in California, testing and ship out of here. So, all that timing worked out well. We’re down the path already with the waiver.

Interestingly enough, the way that whole thing got set up, virtually every single company is going to have to go through that process—which isn’t a bad thing. It’s essentially an audit every year to make sure that drones are being shipped to the United States are going to be safe, that there’s not going to be a national security risk. We’re hopeful we can get through that pretty quickly. In the second quarter of this year, we’re going to be doing what we call beta site installations with several customers, and across the board we have three different segments we address: we address the public safety market, we address agriculture, and then the third one is what I would call traditional commercial applications, like linear inspection and mapping. And there’s a huge amount of that out there that’s going on today.

The post Will Drones Become Part of Critical Infrastructure? appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: Trade Policy Uncertainty: Insights from a Roundtable
item.dc:creator: Ellison Winger
item.description:
The United States has benefited from the opportunity to shape a trade governance that extends well beyond tariffs. International rules on standards, intellectual property, services, customs procedures, and dispute settlement provide a stabilizing framework on which businesses rely.

The post Trade Policy Uncertainty: Insights from a Roundtable appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
On February 12, my colleague Heather Berry and I moderated a roundtable at the Rayburn House Office Building on trade policy uncertainty and its effects on businesses. The roundtable was part of the “Georgetown on the Hill” series organized by the Center for Business and Public Policy at the McDonough School of Business. Panelists included Angela Ellard, Nonresident Senior Advisor at the Center for Strategic and International Studies and former Deputy Director General of the WTO, Nuno Limao, Wallenberg Chair Professor at Georgetown University, and Sarah Wells, CEO and founder of Sarah Wells Bags. In this post, I would like to highlight some of the main takeaways from the event.

The discussion suggested that we have moved from a world where uncertainty centered on tariff levels to one where it increasingly concerns the rules themselves—how they are set, how they change, and whether they will be enforced consistently over time.

Uncertainty shapes behavior even before policies take full effect. Firms invest in advance—placing orders, building supplier relationships, qualifying inputs, or expanding capacity—without knowing what costs or market access will look like months or years ahead. When policy shifts are broad, frequent, or unpredictable, research has shown that businesses delay or scale back those investments. The economic effects can accumulate gradually, even if they are not immediately visible in headline data.

Uncertainty about trade rules affects not only importers but also exporters. Firms must assess whether foreign markets will remain accessible, whether standards or other requirements will change, and whether trade agreements—current or newly negotiated—will provide durable assurance. Even without formal retaliation, doubts about durability can alter expectations and investment plans.

On the ground, firms experience uncertainty as a series of costly operational choices. Even relatively simple supply chains require months of preparation: safety testing, orders, payments, and shipping all occur long before goods reach a U.S. port. When tariffs or enforcement conditions change midstream, firms may face immediate cash demands and difficult decisions about pricing, inventory, and financing. Diversifying production is costly and complex, involving new supplier networks, redesigned inputs, and significant upfront investment—often without confidence that the new arrangement will remain stable.

For small and mid-sized firms, these constraints can be particularly tight. Limited cash reserves and narrow margins amplify the impact of unexpected policy changes. Lost sales from delayed or reduced inventory compound tariff costs. Although higher uncertainty can dampen foreign competition, fewer new entrants also signal that margins and predictability are insufficient to justify investment.

In this environment, trade policy becomes harder to align with broader goals—whether manufacturing expansion, revenue generation, or strategic competition—because uncertainty itself can deter the investment those goals require.

Existing institutions face challenges and a call for reform. Yet, the United States has benefited from the opportunity to shape a trade governance that extends well beyond tariffs. International rules on standards, intellectual property, services, customs procedures, and dispute settlement provide a stabilizing framework on which businesses rely.

The post Trade Policy Uncertainty: Insights from a Roundtable appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: Ending Improper Government Payments to Dead People
item.dc:creator: Ellison Winger
item.category: Ending Improper Payments to Deceased People Act
item.description:
Last week, President Donald Trump signed into law the “Ending Improper Payments to Deceased People Act.” The new law permanently authorizes data sharing between the Social Security Administration’s “Death Master File” and the Department of the Treasury’s “Do Not Pay” list.

The post Ending Improper Government Payments to Dead People appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
Last week, President Donald Trump signed into law the “Ending Improper Payments to Deceased People Act.” You read that correctly. The bipartisan legislation, which was introduced by Sen. John Kennedy (R-LA), updates his prior legislation that temporarily authorized the sharing of death data with benefit-paying agencies. The new law permanently authorizes data sharing between the Social Security Administration’s “Death Master File” and the Department of the Treasury’s “Do Not Pay” list, which is used by various benefit-paying agencies.

One of the cosponsors of the new law, Sen. Ashley Moody (R-FL), describes its intent as “rein(ing) in the government’s ability to make improper payments to deceased people in the future.” As that suggests, government payments to dead people are an ongoing problem. Just in the past two months, the fraud-focused New York Post has featured the following headlines: “California collected millions for 94,000 dead people’s phone and internet services, bombshell FCC report finds” (January 28, 2026); “Trump admin probes Colorado after 221 dead people received federal housing assistance” (December 18, 2025); and “Heartless Calif. welfare worker stole $40K in benefits using identities of elderly, dead people: feds” (December 16, 2025).

Early in the pandemic, official reports noted that stimulus checks worth $1.4 billion were sent to dead people. In 2022, the Department of Labor Inspector General reported that “205,766 Social Security numbers of deceased persons were used to file claims” for pandemic unemployment benefits, resulting in almost $140 million in potential fraud. In December 2025, the Government Accountability Office reported that $94 million in Obamacare tax credits may have been paid to deceased individuals in 2023. Perhaps most famously, earlier last year President Trump and Elon Musk incorrectly suggested that significant Social Security benefits had been paid to deceased individuals.

Sometimes benefits start flowing while a claimant is alive and wrongly continue after death. Take the government’s Lifeline program, which provides nearly $1 billion per year to help low-income Americans afford basic phone and internet services. Last month, the Inspector General of the Federal Communications Commission (FCC), which oversees the program, reported on three states (California, Texas, and Oregon) that had opted out of the national system that checks whether program benefits flow to deceased individuals. Between late 2020 and 2025, providers in those three states claimed funding for over 116,000 subscribers recorded as deceased on the Treasury Department’s Do Not Pay registry. Of those cases, 66 percent involved individuals who died after becoming eligible, yet their benefits continued for several months after their demise.

In more troubling cases, the date of death occurred before a claimant enrolled in benefits. In the three opt-out states profiled in the Inspector General’s report, 15 percent of cases flagged were known to have a date of death before enrollment, while another 19 percent may have died before enrollment. Overall, 81 percent of Lifeline claims involving “deceased subscribers” across the three states were in California.

As that suggests, many of those wrongly receiving assistance are using the identities of dead people to access benefits. The identity of the same deceased individual was sometimes associated with benefit claims in multiple states. The Inspector General followed that trail to also find 270,000 cases in which living individuals claimed benefits two or more times in the same month in California, Texas, and Oregon and other states.

In November, FCC Chairman Brendan Carr revoked California’s opt-out status. That move followed Governor Gavin Newsom’s signing of legislation “that puts California on the path to becoming the first U.S. state offering undocumented immigrants access” to Lifeline benefits, as Politico reported. Sen. Ted Cruz (R-TX), who chairs the Senate Commerce, Science, and Transportation Committee, assailed the California legislation, saying it “likely violates federal law” and “is the latest in a series of measures by Democrat-run jurisdictions that not only incentivize illegal entry into the United States but also obstruct the lawful operations of federal immigration authorities.”

Earlier this week, the FCC adopted a reform proposal to prevent “rampant abuse of the program and its verification processes” including by “ensuring that Lifeline support is used to benefit only legal, living, and eligible Americans.” Even with new federal legislation, that shows how eternal vigilance will likely remain necessary to prevent government benefit payments to dead people.

The post Ending Improper Government Payments to Dead People appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: 2025 Trade and Investment Didn’t Yield Manufacturing Jobs
item.dc:creator: Ellison Winger
item.description:
The year’s trade deficit trend and a key measure of foreign investment in the US provide figures President Trump is likely to emphasize. However, the more optimistic his spin, the more glaring is the poor payoff: manufacturing employment still fell.

The post 2025 Trade and Investment Didn’t Yield Manufacturing Jobs appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
Recently released data covering the whole of 2025 contain good news. The trend for the trade deficit over the course of the year and one measure of foreign investment into the US both offer numbers President Trump will try to highlight. While the extent of the good news can certainly be debated, that isn’t the President’s problem. Instead, the more optimistic his spin, the more glaring is the poor payoff: manufacturing employment still fell.

Trade wins the headlines. The combined American goods and services deficit fell only slightly in 2025, remaining large at $900 billion. But this includes a rush of imports in the first quarter in advance of expected tariffs. After the first quarter, the goods and services deficit fell 26 percent compared to the equivalent period of 2024.

Within that, goods trade is most important because that’s where tariffs were applied and because goods trade is what should affect manufacturing jobs. The goods result is less impressive without services but similar. The 2025 goods deficit is slightly higher than in 2024 but, after tariffs were announced in early April, the last three quarters of last year saw a 16 percent drop compared to the year before.

We also have country data for goods trade. The top 11 (to include Canada, since President Trump is obsessed) national sources of our 2025 goods trade deficit show pronounced changes versus 2024:

Goods Trade Deficits, $ billion


At first blush, the China drop is encouraging. The US should be less dependent on China, and send over less hard currency. But deficit surges with Taiwan and Vietnam are suspicious in the same light. The long-standing use of China as a platform by Taiwanese firms suggests they can just re-label shipments. Uncertainty in Vietnam-US trade relations surprisingly didn’t matter, possibly because China is heavily invested in consumer goods output in Vietnam.

Monthly trade deficits with Taiwan and Vietnam rose over the course of 2025. So did that with Thailand, another easy transshipment route for Chinese products. Meanwhile, there was no deterioration in the Mexico and Ireland deficits over the course of the year (the Ireland deficit all but disappeared by December). New Trump nemesis India may surpass old Trump nemesis Japan as a deficit source in 2026.

Another set of 2025 data received less attention but adds to the administration’s simultaneous success and dilemma. The value of foreign portfolio holdings in the US rose nearly $4.9 trillion in 2025. This is not new spending. Nonetheless, it suspiciously fits the President’s talk of $20 trillion in “new investment” and is a sign of American financial success. The UK led with a $700 billion gain, while our archenemy Canada followed with $500 billion.

That’s 12 months of financial success worth trillions and nine months of trade success, at least on Trump’s view. Unfortunately, that’s not the end of the story. The last three quarters of the 2025 also saw manufacturing employment decline each month. The pace of decline last year was slower than 2024 but over 100,000 jobs were still lost, with employment returning to the levels of early 2022. Not what the President ran on.

Manufacturing job loss in 2025 isn’t the end of the story, either. It can take time for trade and financial changes to affect employment. The number one question for administration officials is when—when will employment rise and how much? The US hasn’t seen 13 million manufacturing jobs since 2008—is that out of reach? If so, trade and investment sound and fury looks largely like grandstanding.

The post 2025 Trade and Investment Didn’t Yield Manufacturing Jobs appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: Advancing AI Literacy in the US Workplace
item.dc:creator: Julia Torres
item.description:
The Department of Labor framework is unashamedly instrumental: It aims to help workers safely use AI to stay productive rather than equip them as critical agents in shaping AI governance or contesting its deployment. It supports real economy delivery in a timely manner—as it should for a technology already in widespread use.

The post Advancing AI Literacy in the US Workplace appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
If there is one truism of the so-called information revolution, it is that the rollout and uptake of each new technological iteration in day-to-day business practice occurs faster than the last. It took over half a century for computers to be widely used, three decades for personal computers to arrive on every desk, 20 years for internet applications to become ubiquitous, and 10 years for smartphone apps to dominate. But in just over three short years since the public release of ChatGPT, businesses and employees are being urged to incorporate the use of generative pretrained transformers (GPTs)—AI tools—into their everyday work practice.

To be sure, their incorporation is inevitable given the tremendous productivity benefits on offer. (McKinsey estimates a 0.1–0.6 percentage point addition to annual labor productivity growth through 2040.) There is little doubt that GPTs have revolutionized computer coding activities and improved the throughput and quality of many knowledge workers’ outputs—for example, agents in contact centers. Medical practitioners’ efficiency is increasing with the use of ambient listening tools to take notes during consultations, and care quality is improving with customized summaries and treatment plans provided subsequently to patients.

However, as with all new technologies, caution is necessary. The same tools can lead to decreases in efficiency and quality if used inappropriately, meaning without sufficient human interpretation or outside the bounds of its capabilities. For example, a University of Sydney academic found that a Deloitte report to an Australian government department contained phantom citations and fabricated footnotes due to unchecked GPT use in its preparation. This cost Deloitte up to AUD 300,000 in foregone fees and a lot of public embarrassment. Likewise, court and opponent time was wasted, and a lawyer faced sanctions after a ChatGPT-prepared brief was found to contain entirely fictitious case law, including invented citations and opinions.

Importantly, just as for all other information technology advances, benefits come not from substituting existing tasks but from enhancing already skilled practitioners’ ability to carry out their activities. GPTs can create computer code quickly, but coders still have to understand the tasks for which the code will be used to craft the appropriate prompts and then select the best code from multiple generated examples. Similarly, AI tools may capture consultation records, but medical practitioners must still edit them and check treatment plans generated to ensure they are appropriate for the specific case.

AI application risks come, therefore, not from the GPTs themselves but from inappropriate use. Such risks are best addressed by education, not regulation. Workers need new skills in AI literacy to make the most of GPT-based applications and avoid their pitfalls.

Propitiously, therefore, last week the US Department of Labor released its Artificial Intelligence Literacy Framework. AI literacy is defined as “a foundational set of competencies that enable individuals to use and evaluate AI technologies responsibly, with a primary focus on generative AI, which is increasingly central to the modern workplace.” The aim is “to support a wide range of users working to strengthen AI literacy across the American workforce.”

The framework is voluntary. It defines five foundational content areas: how AI works, common workplace applications, effective prompting, evaluating AI outputs, and responsible and secure use. Seven delivery principles stress adaptability across sectors, experiential learning, and pairing AI use with “human skills” like judgment, creativity, communication, and problem solving. The framework is targeted at states, workforce boards, community colleges, apprenticeships, and employers and is intended as national guidance for workforce and training programs.

The US framework differs from other national and international AI literacy strategies in its focus on workers rather than on the whole population. This is pragmatic given the extensive use (and misuse) of AI already in the US workplace. If the promised productivity gains are to be made as soon as possible in line with wider economic policy objectives, then workers already at the coalface need these relevant skills now. There just isn’t time to fashion a comprehensive AI-in-education strategy focused first on teachers and students, national curricula, and teacher-training reforms, as promulgated by UNESCO.

The US framework also differs in its tight focus on workplace contexts rather than on broader civic, societal, and ethical competencies and its understanding of AI’s social impact and action toward a “sustainable and fair society.” It eschews a European Union–type tight connection to AI governance (e.g., AI Act implementation, digital rights, and algorithmic transparency) and civic, democratic, and rights-based dimensions of AI literacy.

The Department of Labor framework is unashamedly instrumental: It aims to help workers safely use AI to stay productive rather than equip them as critical agents in shaping AI governance or contesting its deployment. It supports real economy delivery in a timely manner—as it should for a technology already in widespread use.

The post Advancing AI Literacy in the US Workplace appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: A Poisoned Chalice at the Federal Reserve
item.dc:creator: Ellison Winger
item.description:
Handling the budget and AI challenges will require skill and flexibility from Kevin Warsh at the Fed’s helm. Above all, it will require the maintenance of central bank independence without interference from the Trump administration. 

The post A Poisoned Chalice at the Federal Reserve appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
In early 2006, when Ben Bernanke replaced Alan Greenspan as the Federal Reserve’s Chair, The Economist ran a memorable and prescient cover. It had Greenspan and Bernanke in a relay race. However, instead of Greenspan handing Bernanke a baton, it had him handing Bernanke a stick of dynamite. The Economist was referring to the US housing market bubble that was about to burst. When that bubble burst in 2008, Bernanke was left with the unenviable task of having to deal with the 2008-2009 Great Economic Recession. 

If Greenspan left Bernanke with one stick of dynamite, it would seem that Jerome Powell will be handing Kevin Warsh two sticks of dynamite when he hands over his leadership of the Fed in May. Not only will Warsh be left with an unsustainable US public finance position with which to contend. He will also be left with an Artificial Intelligence (AI) bubble that is almost certain to burst on his watch. Those two challenges will make it very difficult for Warsh to deliver on the Fed’s dual mandate to promote maximum employment and stable prices. It will also make it very difficult for Warsh to implement his stated intention of reducing the size of the Fed’s bloated balance sheet. 

Start with unsustainable public finances. According to the Congressional Budget Office, the US public deficit is now set to average over six percent of GDP over the next decade or around double the three percent of GDP that would be consistent with public debt stability. In dollar terms, the US budget deficit is set to rise from around $2 trillion this year to a jaw-dropping $3.1 trillion by 2036. As a result, the public debt to GDP ratio is set to rise from around 100 percent at present to an all-time record of 120 percent by 2036. 

It would be an understatement to say that needing to finance a $2 trillion budget deficit and to roll over $9 trillion a year in maturing US Treasury bonds will highly complicate Warsh’s task of managing monetary policy. If he is perceived to be cutting interest rates at an excessively fast pace, as Trump might pressure him to do, he might raise concerns, especially among foreign Treasury bond holders, that the US will try to inflate its way out of its debt problem. In turn, that could invite a bond market and a dollar market crisis that could spill over to the rest of the financial system. Similarly, if Warsh tries to reduce the size of the Fed’s balance sheet, he risks forcing long-term interest rates higher. That would add to the government’s spending on interest payments, which already stand at close to $1 trillion a year. 

If the experience with the bursting of the 2001 dot.com bubble is anything by which to go, the eventual bursting of the AI bubble could mean that Warsh might have to deal with an economic recession relatively early in his term. Such a recession would compound Warsh’s public finance challenge by increasing the size of the budget deficit. 

One reason to fear an AI-induced recession is that today’s US equity valuations are at lofty levels reminiscent of those preceding the bursting of the dot.com bubble. That could presage a sharp stock market decline as occurred when the dot.com bubble burst. Another reason is that the amounts being invested in AI are now similar in size to those that occurred during the internet boom but are occurring in a much shorter time frame. As if to underline this point, it is estimated that last year the $100 to $200 billion invested in AI last year accounted for around one-third of US economic growth. An even higher amount of AI investment is expected this year. 

Simultaneously handling the budget and AI challenges will require considerable skill and flexibility from Warsh at the Fed’s helm. It will also not afford Warsh the luxury of doggedly holding on to his oft-stated views of wanting lower interest rates and a marked reduction in the Fed’s bloated balance sheet. Above all, it will require the maintenance of central bank independence without interference from the Trump administration. 

         

The post A Poisoned Chalice at the Federal Reserve appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: The Political Backlash to Data Centers
item.dc:creator: Julia Torres
item.description:
The backlash against data centers is real, bipartisan, and accelerating. But the moratoria are blunt instruments aimed at a poorly defined target. They capture far more than hyperscale AI facilities, they hand significant regulatory discretion to agencies without clear legislative direction, and they offer no mechanism for addressing the grid and water concerns that motivated them in the first place. Public frustration with data centers reflects genuine questions about who bears the costs of the AI build-out. Those are legitimate questions. Moratoria do not answer them.

The post The Political Backlash to Data Centers appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
The single most dramatic shift in political opinion over the past six months has been the backlash against data centers.

I first noticed the change last autumn while browsing a post from the Facebook Group of my hometown in Central Illinois. A post about a new CyrusOne data center in Springfield had 145 comments, making it one of the more active discussions on the page. Even though the facility would operate on a closed-loop water system, commenters fixated on its water use. And despite the fact that CyrusOne would fund the necessary transmission upgrades and pay the full cost of service, many still feared the project would drive up local energy bills.

My hometown is not alone. National polling shows that public opinion is weakening on data centers. Heatmap’s poll of nearly 4,000 registered voters found that only 44% would support a data center being built near them, while 42% would oppose it, a net approval of just +2%. That put data centers below solar, wind, natural gas, nuclear, and even geothermal in local support. But the most striking stat comes from Morning Consult. In successive polls just a month apart in October and November 2025, support for an outright ban on data center construction grew from 37% to 41%.

This dislike for data centers is starting to translate into political action. According to Data Center Watch, between May 2024 and March 2025, $64 billion in US data center projects were blocked or delayed. In Michigan, at least 19 communities have proposed or implemented data center bans. And to not be outdone, Senator Bernie Sanders has proposed a data center ban.

State legislatures are moving in a similar direction. In just the past few months, lawmakers in Georgia, New York, Oklahoma, Virginia, and Maryland have introduced bills to pause or restrict data center construction. The bills differ in duration and scope, but they all aim to stop building until the costs can be properly studied. The table below maps the current landscape.    

The most aggressive of the bills is from New York State Senator Liz Krueger, who recently proposed S9144. The bill would disallow permits for siting, construction, or operation of data centers until the state completes a full regulatory process which cannot happen sooner than three years after enactment. Importantly, the bill doesn’t specify a date when the regulation has to be completed, so the ban could remain permanent.

This bill also requires the Department of Environmental Conservation to produce a Generic Environmental Impact Statement (GEIS) on data centers within 18 months. A GEIS differs from a standard environmental review. Instead of analyzing the impacts of a single project at a specific site, it assesses impacts across an entire category of development statewide. Once a GEIS is complete, future projects that fall within its scope can be reviewed against its findings rather than starting from scratch, which can speed up individual approvals.

But the efficiency benefit cuts both ways. A GEIS gives the agency substantial authority to define what counts as an acceptable data center, what mitigation measures are required, and what conditions must be met before a project can proceed. If the Department of Environmental Conservation writes a restrictive GEIS, it could function as a de facto regulatory framework for data centers in New York, independent of any further legislative action.

What’s most striking about these bills is that they aren’t directed at the large data centers from Google, Meta, and Amazon. Big tech is mostly building massive data centers, known as hyperscale data centers. According to the common industry definition, hyperscalers use 5,000 servers or more and devote at least 10,000 square feet to the operation, drawing 100 MW or more. Instead, the bills have thresholds that range from 1 MW (Virginia) to 20 MW (New York) to 100 MW (Oklahoma). Virginia’s 1 MW threshold and Georgia’s lack of a MW definition would be especially onerous as it would capture colocation firms, regional telecom operators, university research centers, and government agencies, not just AI companies.

The backlash against data centers is real, bipartisan, and accelerating. But the moratoria are blunt instruments aimed at a poorly defined target. They capture far more than hyperscale AI facilities, they hand significant regulatory discretion to agencies without clear legislative direction, and they offer no mechanism for addressing the grid and water concerns that motivated them in the first place. Public frustration with data centers reflects genuine questions about who bears the costs of the AI build-out. Those are legitimate questions. Moratoria do not answer them.

The post The Political Backlash to Data Centers appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: Rubio in Munich and the Civic Foundations Universities Can’t Ignore
item.dc:creator: Ellison Winger
item.description:
In Munich this week, Marco Rubio spoke of the need to renew the habits that allow free societies to endure—not merely through power, but through civic unity and democratic confidence. But that civic ethic is what many universities now find hardest to sustain.

The post Rubio in Munich and the Civic Foundations Universities Can’t Ignore appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
Speaking in Munich this week, Secretary of State Marco Rubio offered European leaders something more than diplomatic reassurance. He offered a diagnosis of what holds the democratic West together and what happens when those civic foundations weaken. 

Rubio began with an unusually explicit claim: the transatlantic alliance is not merely transactional. It is historical and cultural. The United States and Europe, he argued, are bound by more than security coordination. They are joined by shared values, shared history, and the civilizational inheritance that made liberal democracy possible in the first place. 

“We belong together,” Rubio insisted; not as a matter of convenience, but as a matter of identity. The alliance, he suggested, is rooted in the things that unite us, not only in the threats that confront us. 

That framing matters. Rubio was arguing that free societies thrive and endure only when they collectively possess enough moral confidence and civic cohesion to defend their shared ideals and values. 

And that is where higher education enters the picture. 

Rubio’s speech was animated by a concern that has become impossible to miss across the democratic world: Institutions can survive only if citizens still believe in the legitimacy of the project. Alliances cannot be sustained through bureaucracy alone. Democracies cannot be sustained through procedure alone. They require civic trust, cultural continuity, and the capacity to argue without dissolving into fragmentation. 

Rubio’s emphasis on shared inheritance points toward a question universities once took seriously: Who forms the citizens capable of sustaining democratic unity? 

For much of modern history, higher education understood itself as part of the civic architecture of the West. Universities did not merely credential students. They cultivated the habits Rubio’s speech presupposes—intellectual seriousness, democratic restraint, and an appreciation for the moral achievements of liberal civilization. 

But today, universities increasingly struggle to play that role. 

Rubio warned against a hollowing out of common purpose. His message was that the West cannot remain strong if it loses confidence in the values that undergird it. Yet American higher education has become one of the primary sites where that confidence erodes: where shared inheritance is treated as embarrassment, where national cohesion is framed as exclusion, and where the language of democracy is often turned inward as accusation rather than outward as aspiration. 

Rubio’s remarks were also notable for what they implied about seriousness. Partnership, he suggested, depends on democratic societies being willing to defend sovereignty, borders, and civic order—not as abstractions, but as conditions of self-government. The alliance is not simply a set of commitments on paper; it is a shared willingness to sustain the moral and civic prerequisites of freedom. 

That theme has an uncomfortable resonance on campus. Universities speak constantly about “democracy,” but often hesitate to teach the responsibilities that democracy requires: the obligations of citizenship, the discipline of pluralism, the acceptance of disagreement, the necessity of limits. 

The result is not only ideological imbalance. It is civic thinning. 

One striking feature of Rubio’s Munich speech was his insistence that democratic life depends on persuasion rather than coercion. The West, at its best, governs through argument. Rubio spoke of the need to renew the habits that allow free societies to endure—not merely through power, but through civic unity and democratic confidence. 

But that civic ethic is precisely what many universities now find hardest to sustain. 

According to the latest National Speech Index, nearly three-quarters of Americans now say free speech in the United States is headed in the wrong direction. That pessimism reflects more than partisan mood. It reflects a growing fear that the institutions meant to model democratic argument—above all, universities—are losing the capacity to do so. 

Rubio’s speech was, in this sense, an implicit reminder: You cannot defend democratic unity abroad if you cannot sustain democratic argument at home. 

The deeper danger is not that the transatlantic alliance will fracture because of a single dispute. It is that the cultural foundations of liberal democracy will continue to weaken—leaving societies less able to cooperate, less able to trust, and less able to name what they share. 

Higher education cannot determine foreign policy. But it plays a central role in shaping whether future leaders inherit the civic confidence Rubio appealed to in Munich: The belief that liberal democracy is not merely a system to manage, but a civilization to sustain. 

Rubio’s core claim was simple: The West is more than an arrangement. It is an inheritance. 

If universities cannot teach that inheritance, honestly, pluralistically, and without apology, then the alliance Rubio defended will rest on thinner ground than any treaty can repair. 

The West will not be held together by weapons or wealth, but by whether its schools still teach the civic meaning of the words Rubio used in Munich: “We belong together.” 

The post Rubio in Munich and the Civic Foundations Universities Can’t Ignore appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: A Class President Is Not a Political Faction
item.dc:creator: Ellison Winger
item.description:
Universities often insist they must remain neutral, but neutrality does not mean abdication. If a class president wishes to engage politically, they may do so as a private citizen. Student government exists to represent all students, not to function as an organized faction. 

The post A Class President Is Not a Political Faction appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
I was recently handed a flyer at Sarah Lawrence College advertising a “Community-Building Dinner Fundraiser for Gaza” and “Power Mapping 101,” co-hosted by Students for Justice in Palestine (SJP) and the “Class of 2028 President.” 

This was not a neutral event. It was ideological organizing, framed explicitly in the language of factional power: “Want to know who calls the shots on campus?” 

SJP activism at Sarah Lawrence has not remained within the bounds of ordinary advocacy. In recent months, it has repeatedly crossed into rhetoric that dehumanizes Jews and into tactics of disruption and intimidation that corrode the basic norms of university life and undermine student safety, learning, and expression. 

Of course, students are free to protest. Protest is part of university life. But protest in a liberal private institution exists within limits: it cannot become coercion, obstruction, or degradation. Free expression does not mean intimidation. Advocacy does not mean the capture of representative offices. 

That is what makes this flyer alarming. It is not signed by a private student. It is signed by an office. 

Not a name, but “Class of 2028 President.” 

That changes the meaning entirely. A student may join SJP, fundraise, or organize under their own name. But a class president is not simply another activist. That title carries delegated legitimacy. It signals: I speak for the class. 

And that is precisely the breach here. Student government exists to serve an entire class students of different backgrounds, beliefs, and convictions. Its purpose is representation across disagreement, not alignment with a faction. 

A student in the class of 2028 who supports Israel, is ambivalent, or simply does not wish to affiliate with SJP politics now has a class president who has publicly aligned the office with one side of a polarizing and morally charged conflict. That student did not lose a debate. They lost representation. They learn, without being told, that some politics counts as “community,” while theirs is treated as suspect. 

That is not inclusion. It is symbolic conscription. 

To my knowledge, the Class of 2028 did not vote to affiliate itself with SJP. It did not consent to having its elected leadership function as an arm of a political movement. The title borrows institutional legitimacy. It transforms private activism into an act of representation. 

And this is not occurring in a vacuum. Last year, Gaza protests at Sarah Lawrence culminated in the occupation of Westlands, the college’s main administrative building, followed by a week-long encampment—widely reported because it crossed from expression into sustained obstruction of operations.
