In January, SJP-affiliated students shouted down New York Times columnist Ezra Klein—himself a critic of Israel—while the college president sat onstage in silence. That was not dialogue. It was disruption as a political weapon, met with administrative passivity. Universities cannot pretend such tactics are merely “speech.” 

The flyer’s promise of “Power Mapping 101” is revealing. Power mapping is not civic education. It is organizing instruction: identify who holds authority, apply leverage, seize institutional control. The language of “who calls the shots” is not the language of community. It is the language of capture. 

When elected student offices lend their names to that project, the university is no longer dealing with ordinary activism. It is lending representative authority to a factional movement. And when an institution allows intimidation to be renamed as “community-building,” it does not remain neutral. It legitimizes menace. 

Universities have developed a dangerous reflex: proceduralizing antisemitism into oblivion. When Jewish students report being degraded, administrators reach for euphemism —“tensions,” “difficult conversations,” “stakeholder engagement.” Documented instances of dehumanizing rhetoric are not misunderstandings. They are lines being crossed. 

And the chilling effect is measurable. According to the Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression, 166 of 257 campuses received a failing grade in the 2026 College Free Speech Rankings and student self-censorship has risen every year since tracking began. Not because speech is formally banned, but because campus climates increasingly punish dissent. 

That is not the marketplace of ideas. It is factional enforcement. 

Universities often insist they must remain neutral. Fine. But neutrality does not mean abdication. If a class president wishes to engage politically, they may do so as a private citizen, under their own name. The university must clarify that student government exists to represent all students, not to function as a megaphone for an organized faction. 

The issue is not whether students may protest. The issue is whether the institution still knows the difference between protest and menace, between advocacy and coercion, between private activism and representative authority. 

Equal justice and equal representation are not metaphors. They are obligations. Universities do not get to outsource institutional legitimacy to factional movements and call it “community-building.” A university that does so cannot still claim to be an institution of liberal education. 

The post A Class President Is Not a Political Faction appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: A Civic Crisis Schools Can Fix
item.dc:creator: Ellison Winger
item.description:
For too many young Americans today, disagreement is treated not as a normal part of learning but as a threat—something to be avoided, silenced, or managed. This is more than a cultural mood. It is a civic vulnerability. 

The post A Civic Crisis Schools Can Fix appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
For too many young Americans today, disagreement is treated not as a normal part of learning but as a threat—something to be avoided, silenced, or managed. This is more than a cultural mood. It is a civic vulnerability. 

According to the Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression’s latest National Speech Index, nearly three-quarters of Americans say free speech is headed in the wrong direction. That pessimism is rising, not falling, even as Americans continue to affirm the First Amendment in principle. 

That is why the work underway at Birch Wathen Lenox (BWL), a New York K–12 school, deserves national attention. BWL is treating constructive dialogue not as a slogan or an assembly theme, but as a serious educational model: a developmental arc that teaches students, year by year, how to engage disagreement with trust, compassion, and evidence. 

This is not indoctrination. It is education. 

In Lower School, it may mean learning how to listen through stories about fairness. In Middle School, it means practicing perspective-taking and evaluating sources. In Upper School, it means wrestling seriously with contested questions – economic systems, scientific tradeoffs, cultural debates—anchored in texts and disciplined inquiry rather than social media heat. 

Students are not rewarded for performative outrage or instant certainty. They are rewarded for fairness, evidence, and intellectual honesty—for the difficult work of articulating the strongest version of an argument they may reject. One of the central practices is “steel-manning,” not straw-manning: learning to understand before refuting, to clarify before condemning. 

The goal is not consensus; it is capacity. 

For two decades as a teacher of politics, I’ve watched students wrestle with moral conflict and competing visions of justice. But in recent years something has shifted: many young people arrive having absorbed the lesson that disagreement is danger, that hard questions are harm, and that silence is safer than engagement. 

This is not simply about politics. It is about the information environment in which students are being formed. Social media trains them to treat every conversation as a performance, every disagreement as a threat to belonging, every moment of uncertainty as weakness. The incentives are all wrong: hot takes travel faster than careful argument, and moral labeling replaces persuasion. 

That is not sustainable. A democracy cannot function if citizens cannot tolerate contestation. 

Constructive dialogue is not merely a classroom technique. It is a school of character. It requires courage—the willingness to speak honestly under social pressure. It requires humility—the ability to admit uncertainty, to say “I don’t know,” or “I was wrong.” It requires charity—the discipline of interpreting an opponent’s argument in its strongest form rather than its weakest. And it requires self-command—the ability to remain steady when emotions rise. 

These are not soft virtues; they are the virtues that free societies depend on. 

Tocqueville understood this early. American democracy, he observed, rested not only on laws or institutions, but on the habits and mores of the people—the quiet forms of restraint and civic practice that make freedom sustainable. 

Schools are where those habits are formed. 

One of the most important lessons students can learn today is also one of the simplest: the ability to say, plainly, “I don’t know.” In an age saturated with instant answers, recognizing the limits of one’s knowledge is not weakness. It is the beginning of inquiry. Our culture increasingly trains students to perform certainty rather than practice reflection. Schools should do the opposite. 

A crucial part of BWL’s model is institutional neutrality. Teachers are facilitators, not advocates. The school does not exist to deliver official conclusions on contested political questions, but to equip students to reason through those questions themselves. Neutrality is not indifference. It is discipline. It creates the conditions for genuine inquiry rather than conformity. 

This is not a boutique innovation. It is a recovery of something older and essential: the idea that schools form citizens by forming habits. We teach math through repetition, writing through revision, music through practice. We must teach democratic disagreement the same way – through structured encounters with complexity, guided by adults who model restraint and fairness. 

This is not work reserved for one kind of community or student. Every school that claims to prepare young people for adult life in a pluralistic society has reason to take this seriously. The alternative is leaving students to learn conflict from the internet. 

Schools cannot solve polarization alone. But they can do something essential: form young people with the virtues that make democratic life possible. 

The most dangerous outcome is not that students will disagree. It is that they will become incapable of disagreement without fear. 

Freedom of speech is not a slogan. It is a civic discipline. And BWL is showing where that discipline must begin. 

The post A Civic Crisis Schools Can Fix appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: Housing Doesn’t Need Higher Prices—It Needs More Homes
item.dc:creator: Ellison Winger
item.description:
As housing affordability dominates national headlines, policymakers from the White House to Capitol Hill are scrambling for answers—but focusing on rising prices alone obscures the deeper problem: an entrenched shortage of homes. 

The post Housing Doesn’t Need Higher Prices—It Needs More Homes appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
As housing affordability dominates national headlines, policymakers from the White House to Capitol Hill are scrambling for answers—but focusing on rising prices alone obscures the deeper problem: an entrenched shortage of homes. 

Home prices rising isn’t automatically a sign of a healthy housing market. When appreciation runs far ahead of incomes for years, it stops looking like wealth creation and starts acting like a quiet tax on everyone who didn’t buy early. 

The current administration’s economic assumptions—including expectations of lower mortgage rates and strong economic growth—point toward a housing market outcome that deserves closer scrutiny. Under those conditions, and holding supply constraints constant, our estimates suggest that home prices could rise by roughly 10 percent per year. That isn’t automatically a sign of success. 

When it comes to home price appreciation, it’s best to avoid extremes—neither too much nor too little. Appreciation that runs far ahead of incomes for prolonged periods creates its own set of problems. 

As a country, we have recent experience with what sustained appreciation looks like. Since 2012, national home prices have risen by roughly 144 percent, while household incomes have increased by only 64 percent. Unsurprisingly, housing affordability has become one of the most salient political issues in the country. From that perspective alone, it’s worth questioning whether rapid home-price appreciation should be treated as a policy success. 

Yet that kind of price growth is often framed as an unambiguous positive—a wealth effect that boosts household balance sheets and consumer confidence. But home price appreciation isn’t free wealth. When prices rise much faster than incomes for extended periods, the downsides accumulate and affect far more people than is commonly acknowledged, including many homeowners themselves. 

Start with the macro picture. Rapid home-price appreciation increases boom-and-bust risk and diverts capital toward existing housing rather than productive investment. It reduces labor mobility, making it harder for workers to move to opportunity and for employers—especially in high-cost regions—to hire and retain staff. Over time, it widens inequality, particularly across generations, as younger households face higher barriers to entry while older cohorts benefit largely from timing. 

Always-renters are the most obvious losers. House prices and rents are linked in the long run, but prices typically adjust faster. In supply-constrained markets, rent growth often follows price growth with a lag, leaving renters facing higher housing costs without benefiting from the appreciating asset. 

Renters who hope to buy eventually are in a similar position. Rapid appreciation raises purchase prices and requires down payments, forcing households to save longer or take on more leverage. In practice, this delays homeownership or shuts the door to ownership altogether. 

The story for current homeowners is more complicated—and often misunderstood. 

Rapid home-price appreciation also undermines many homeowners—especially those policymakers often assume are benefiting. For households hoping to move up, higher prices raise both the value of their current home and the cost of the next one, often widening the financing gap rather than closing it. For young families, the shortage of starter and move-up homes makes it harder to find the extra bedroom that would support having another child, turning housing into a constraint on family formation. And for older homeowners, rising values frequently translate into higher property taxes, insurance, and maintenance costs, while taxes, transaction costs, and limited downsizing options make it difficult to tap accumulated equity. The result is a growing class of homeowners who look wealthier on paper but are increasingly cash-constrained in practice. 

Only one group consistently benefits from rapid home-price appreciation: homeowners who plan to sell and downsize, particularly if they can move to a lower-cost area. But even here, the upside is frequently eroded by taxes and the scarcity of suitable housing options. 

This is why the idea that building more homes would “crater the market” gets housing policy backwards. Stable prices supported by adequate supply are a sign of a healthy housing market, not a failing one. When supply is allowed to respond to demand, price growth moderates, affordability improves, and households gain flexibility rather than paper wealth alone. 

More homes mean more options—for renters trying to buy, for families needing room to grow, and for older homeowners looking to downsize without punitive tax consequences. They also mean less pressure to rely on ever-rising prices to generate wealth, and fewer incentives for speculative behavior that thrives in artificially scarce markets. 

The Goldilocks principle applies here: The goal shouldn’t be falling prices, but neither should it be prices that race far ahead of incomes year after year. Housing markets don’t need ever-higher prices—they need enough homes. 

The post Housing Doesn’t Need Higher Prices—It Needs More Homes appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: The Justice Department Could Break the Federal Contracting System. That Might Be a Good Thing.
item.dc:creator: Ellison Winger
item.description:
Federal contractors face a new threat to their business models as the Trump Administration’s Department of Justice rediscovered that the False Claims Act can be weaponized to enforce policies the executive branch wishes to impose upon firms that contract with the government. 

The post The Justice Department Could Break the Federal Contracting System. That Might Be a Good Thing. appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
Federal contractors face a new threat to their business models as the Trump Administration’s Department of Justice (DOJ) appears to have rediscovered what past Administrations have long known. The False Claims Act (FCA) can be weaponized and used to enforce whatever policies the executive branch wishes to impose upon those firms who choose to contract with the government. How this power is ultimately used and how companies and Congress react will determine whether this is a good idea or not.

The FCA was enacted in 1863 during the height of the Civil War. The so-called Lincoln Law was designed to ferret out contract fraud that was rampant at the time. Examples of shoddy goods (originally, low-quality materials that caused uniforms to melt in the rain), rancid meat, and gunpowder filled with sawdust compelled the government to create more effective tools to counter massive fraud. In addition to increasing the prosecutorial power of the government, the new law empowered individual citizens under the 13th century concept of qui tam to report on wrongdoing, sue on behalf of the government and obtain a portion of the damages. The FCA became a successful tool in the battle against genuine contract fraud—be it financial, product substitution, bid rigging, bribery, or kickbacks.

The problem is that in the modern era the definition of contract fraud began to widen as socioeconomic experimentation in contracting gained prominence. Government contractors have incrementally been forced to comply with a glut of government unique socio-economic, oversight, labor, and environmental policies contained in federal contracts. Each of these mandates once embedded in a contract clause became the basis for future fraud claims under the FCA. The threat of prosecution for any non-compliance of these clauses marked an escalation of using false claims beyond what many would define as “real” contract fraud by applying it to enforce political and social aspirations and goals. 

The Act eventually became a self-licking ice cream cone designed to perpetuate progressive values as firms acted to preclude lawsuits. The left hoped to infect the commercial non-governmental facing portions of companies whose subsidiaries had federal contracts and force these firms to apply non-market social mandates to their entire organization. What they didn’t count on is that businesses are free agents. As non-commercial mandates grew and the 1986 amendments to FCA made it even easier to use it as a tool of progressive politics, commercial companies abandoned the federal market in droves. 

Not surprisingly, without these firms the government fell behind the private sector in keeping up with innovation. The history of government acquisition reform since the early 1990s has been an attempt to bring back those commercial companies who had left the market and entice new emerging technology companies to work with the government. In 1994, Congress specifically exempted commercial companies from most government unique socioeconomic mandates, partially shielding them from the FCA. This worked for about 15 years until what once were a limited handful of government unique commercial contracting clauses exploded under the Obama and Biden Administrations. 

The Trump Administration has now seemingly figured out the secret of exploiting the FCA and is just beginning to use it to apply to contractors as a way to enforce its own vision of DEI, tariff, environmental, and labor policies. Commercial contractors such as Google and Verizon are now reported to being investigated by DOJ under the FCA to change their diversity hiring practices. No doubt many more such uses are on the way.

A demonstration of government overreach using a legal tool that the left has exploited for decades just might be what is needed to establish the case to reform the FCA and place restrictions on this particular arbitrary use of government power. The danger is this Administration will grow too fond of the FCA and dream up new contract clauses that conform to its ideology and policy goals. 

This could break the system and undermine what limited progress that has been made in widening the defense industrial base in the past decade. The risks of government contracting may become too high for those firms with only a limited amount of government sales and facing conflicting mandates depending on what Administration a contract was awarded. As was the case in the 1970–80s, these firms can exit the federal market to detriment of our national security.

A better alternative exists. Congress can act and limit the applicability of the FCA to only instances of unquestionable and genuine contract fraud. Another approach would be to eliminate and prohibit all instances of non-commercial socioeconomic clauses in federal contacts so as not to trigger the FCA in the future. If current DOJ actions lead to such reforms, then the short-term disruption to the system would be well worth it.

The post The Justice Department Could Break the Federal Contracting System. That Might Be a Good Thing. appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: Parents over Platforms: Why App-Level Responsibility Is the Smarter Path for Kids’ Online Safety
item.dc:creator: Julia Torres
item.description:
To help protect children online, lawmakers should resist the lure of centralized symbolism and instead place responsibility where it can make a difference—at the application level, where design decisions shape behavior and outcomes every day.

The post Parents over Platforms: Why App-Level Responsibility Is the Smarter Path for Kids’ Online Safety appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
I’ve written about this before, but it bears repeating: Should responsibility for kids’ online safety lie with operating systems and app stores, or with the applications themselves? At first glance, the operating system (OS) approach seems appealing. Verify a child’s age at the device’s operating level, block downloads deemed inappropriate by regulation, and call it a day. It sounds clean, efficient, and centralized. But it is also insufficient.

The core problem is that operating systems are distribution channels, while applications are experiences. When it comes to children’s online safety, the risk lies in the experience, not in access. An app store can only gate the initial access. It does not control design. Once a child downloads an application, the operating system no longer shapes what happens next. It does not determine whether an app uses algorithmically amplified feeds. It does not control whether push notifications are engineered to maximize engagement. It does not design in-app messaging features or decide how user data is collected and monetized. Developers make those decisions.

Children do not experience the operating system; they use individual apps. Consider how kids use devices today. As I have noted previously, on the same phone, a teenager might have a messaging app, a short-form video platform with algorithmic recommendations, a fitness tracker, and a restaurant-ordering app. All four apps are delivered initially through the same app store, yet their risk profiles differ dramatically.

A video platform optimized for engagement, driven by recommendation engines and social amplification, raises entirely different concerns than an app used to order takeout. Treating these services as interchangeable because they share a distribution channel overlooks the fundamental distinction between device-level access and application-level design.

Broad mandates imposed at the operating system level risk creating a false sense of security among parents while imposing compliance burdens on low-risk applications. That outcome would neither enhance safety nor promote innovation. Plus, the modern digital environment is inherently multi-device. Even if an OS-level age gate worked perfectly on a smartphone, it would not follow a child across devices. The same application may be available on a gaming console, a laptop, a tablet, or a smart TV.

More importantly, a one-time verification at download does not account for how applications evolve. Children’s online experiences are dynamic, and apps are not static products; they update frequently as features are introduced, tested, modified, and occasionally retired. Algorithms change, and monetization strategies often shift as the app’s use evolves.

If Congress wants durable protections, it must recognize that safety obligations should track with the product’s ongoing design choices, not with a one-time transaction at the distribution layer. Application-level responsibility aligns capability with accountability because the developer controls the app’s content moderation systems, data collection practices, recommendation algorithms, and engagement mechanisms. They are uniquely positioned to implement age-appropriate safeguards because they build and continuously refine features that may pose risks.

When responsibility sits with developers, incentives align. If a company introduces a feature that increases risk for minors, it must address that risk directly. If a service poses minimal risk, such as a restaurant app or a basic productivity tool, it is not swept into compliance regimes designed for algorithmically driven social platforms.

Application-level safeguards also empower parents more effectively. Such an approach places responsibility where it belongs: with those who design the user experience. When protections are built into specific services, families can make informed decisions about individual products. Parents can evaluate what an app does, what behaviors it encourages, what data it collects, and what risks it poses. That level of transparency is impossible when everything is filtered through a single operating system checkpoint.

None of this argues against congressional action. On the contrary, Congress should act to protect children online. If Congress gets this right, it can align responsibility with capability, preserve innovation in low-risk applications, ensure high-risk features are appropriately managed, and create a framework flexible enough to adapt to evolving technology.

To help protect children online, lawmakers should resist the lure of centralized symbolism and instead place responsibility where it can make a difference—at the application level, where design decisions shape behavior and outcomes every day.

The post Parents over Platforms: Why App-Level Responsibility Is the Smarter Path for Kids’ Online Safety appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: The Age of AI Depends Less on Voters Than on Volts
item.dc:creator: Ellison Winger
item.description:
The biggest thing that could slow AI isn’t politics or local protests, it’s whether there’s enough electricity available to power those warehouse-sized supercomputers. Now that’s something that should be front of mind for America’s political class.

The post The Age of AI Depends Less on Voters Than on Volts appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
No AI data centers, no AI revolution.

Or to be less dramatic, slowing the buildout of these sprawling server farms will slow technical advances and the economywide spread of generative artificial intelligence, which is shaping up to be a powerful new general-purpose technology. As such, a new survey from Politico suggests Silicon Valley shouldn’t take voter tolerance for granted.

Let’s start with the good news for AI companies: Just 28 percent of 2,000 surveyed would oppose the building of a new data center in their area. That, versus 37 percent who would support construction and 28 percent who would neither support nor oppose. 

Then things get complicated. Public opinion turns out to be extremely sensitive to electricity costs. Among those initially supportive, nearly two-thirds would still back a local data center if it raised their monthly power bill by just $5. But that backing erodes quickly as the price tag climbs. At a $10 increase, support slips to about half. By $25, opponents outnumber supporters. And if bills rose by $50 a month, nearly six in 10 would oppose the project, while only about one-third would still favor it.

Then there’s the Trump factor: Tie expansion to the US president and backing among Democrats (or at least Kamala Harris voters) and current undecideds falls roughly 20 points. Oh, and of course, the NIMBY factor: Only 37 percent support a facility within three miles of home.

These survey results are likely of little surprise to Big Tech, given events happening on the ground:

  • In Prince William County, Va.—part of the region’s major data-center hub—residents have organized against rapid expansion, citing round-the-clock low-frequency noise from industrial cooling systems, pollution risk from the heavy use of diesel backup generators, and the need for new transmission lines and substations near neighborhoods. 
  • In Howell Township, Mich., a proposed roughly $1 billion data-center project tied to Meta was withdrawn after residents mobilized against it and the township imposed a temporary moratorium on new data-center development.
  • In Bessemer, Ala., residents objected to forest clearing, heavy water use, and disputed job projections tied to a $14.5 billion data-center campus described as “the size of 18 Walmarts,” notes The New York Times.

So, does this early polling and nascent public opposition signal big trouble ahead for the AI infrastructure buildout? 

Probably not, at least according to Goldman Sachs. The bank says in a recent analysis that local pushback usually slows projects or pushes them to other locations, rather than stopping them altogether. Even moratoriums “have not changed data center development patterns,” and outright bans would likely mean relocation “rather than cancellations.” Also: Clearer regulation could reduce uncertainty and enable clearer and faster approvals.

True, lawmakers are trying to shift more electricity costs onto data centers, but Goldman expects higher power prices to have “limited impact on future data center power demand growth.” There’s just so much need.

In the end, the bank concludes, the biggest thing that could slow AI isn’t politics or local protests, it’s whether there’s enough electricity available to power those warehouse-sized supercomputers. Now that’s something that should be front of mind for America’s political class.

The post The Age of AI Depends Less on Voters Than on Volts appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: What ​​Bari Weiss Can Learn from PBS. Really​.
item.dc:creator: Ellison Winger
item.description:
America does not need another newscast with a photogenic anchor and a mix of the same stories as every other news program. A newscast that combines drama and substance might actually get viewers to tune in. 

The post What ​​Bari Weiss Can Learn from PBS. Really​. appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
Bari Weiss has promised “to make CBS News fit for purpose in the 21st century.”  The conventional expectation has been that she will push the CBS Evening News, the network’s flagship program, toward the Right, a la Fox News. ​​Indeed, she’s said to have tried to lure Bret Baire of Fox to CBS.  

Here’s ​​a radical suggestion for her, even as CBS News producers take buyouts en masses:​​ Take a lesson from PBS. ​​Admittedly, it’s not an obvious suggestion.​ ​​​The PBS News Hour struggles to reach a million viewers, most of whom are over 65. Its story selection has a consistent anti-Trump spin. Its stories go​​ on three times longer than they should.  

But the core idea, inherited from Robert MacNeill and Jim Lehrer decades ago, could distinguish CBS from its competitors and inject excitement—by combining the news with fast-paced ​​interviews that will themselves make news.   

America does not need another newscast with a photogenic anchor (Tony Dokopuil) and a mix of the same stories as every other news program (eg. Nancy Guthrie, ice storms, Kristi Noem fires her pilot). It’s not working so far. According to ​AdWeek​, “CBS Evening News drew 4.538 million total viewers and 623,000 viewers in the key Adults 25-54 demo for the week of January 26. This was down by seven percent in total viewers and down eight percent in the demo compared to the week prior.”   

In other words, it’s time for a radical intervention—not aspirin but CRISPR-based innovation. Instead, Weiss—as she’s done so well at her Free Press site—should inject the informative drama of the old 60 Minutes “point-counterpoint” mini-debates ​​into every newscast. Inflation news? Have Scott Bessent tangle with Paul Krugman​.​​​ Tariffs? Cue Howard Luttnick and Phil Gramm. Repeal of ​the ​carbon dioxide endangerment ​finding​​​? Cue Bill McKibben vs. Lee Zeldin. Hype the confrontations in advance: Steven Miller squaring off against the owner of a chicken processing plant losing its illegal immigrant workforce. ​​In other words, combine journalism and that​​ Trump favorite, Ultimate Fighting. Short but not sweet.  

Weiss has already proven ​​she is accomplished at interview “gets”—as per her extended interview with Erika Kirk and the Tony Dokoupil interview with JD Vance. ​​But these need not be stand​​alone programs. Better to incorporate their sound bite fireworks into the nightly news—both to make news (as do the Sunday morning interview shows) and to distinguish the former Walter Cronkite approach from its contemporary competitors. 

Three mini-debates a night would add content—and pizazz​.​​​ But that should not preclude original reporting. Every newscast could include a Report from America—not a cute shaggy dog story but ​​a story about a local controversy (all news must have drama) ​​that carries broader meaning. ​​A local zoning dispute​​ reveals how hard it ​​is to build new housing.  A “harm reduction” drug control strategy paired with neighbors who don’t want addicts on their street corners. Or culture pieces: the revival of Cajun culture in Louisiana, the decline of Broadway attendance. Or ideas this older American doesn’t know anything about.​​  

The reason CBS trails its competitors at 6:30 pm isn’t just what’s been it​​s liberal bias but the fact that there hasn’t been a dime’s worth of difference among the​​ three legacy networks’ approach.   

A newscast that combines drama and substance might actually get viewers to tune in. 

The post What ​​Bari Weiss Can Learn from PBS. Really​. appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: Suicides, Settlements, and Unresolved Chatbot Issues: A Long Litigation Road Lies Ahead
item.dc:creator: Julia Torres
item.description:
Prospective settlements in several lawsuits blaming conversational chatbots for causing minors to kill themselves aren’t likely to stop related claims from proceeding, with multiple forces driving litigation and settlements failing to resolve key issues.

The post Suicides, Settlements, and Unresolved Chatbot Issues: A Long Litigation Road Lies Ahead appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
News broke last month that settlements may soon arrive in several high-profile lawsuits—Garcia v. Character Technologies and Montoya v. Character Technologies, included—that blame companies and individuals behind or allegedly associated with Character.AI chatbots for causing minors to kill themselves. Defendants likely settling are Character Technologies (the company operating Character.AI) and two Character.AI co-founders (Noam Shazeer and Daniel De Freitas Adiwarsana), plus Google and its parent company, Alphabet.

Character.AI and the Social Media Victims Law Center (SMVLC), a law firm representing the plaintiffs, issued a joint statement asserting that “Character.AI has taken innovative and decisive steps with regard to AI safety and teens, and will continue to champion these efforts and push others across the industry to adopt similar safety standards.”

That’s good news, but don’t expect lawsuits blaming conversational and companionship chatbots for allegedly provoking the tragic actions of—and causing harm to or the deaths of—both minors and adults to suddenly disappear. SMVLC filed seven lawsuits in California in November against OpenAI (the company behind ChatGPT) and its CEO, Sam Altman. Also in the mix are recently filed cases targeting those same defendants filed by the Edelson firm, including First County Bank v. OpenAI Foundation and Raine v. OpenAI.

Raine is brought by the parents of 16-year-old Adam Raine, who killed himself after ChatGPT allegedly “became Adam’s closest confidant,” validated “his most harmful and self-destructive thoughts,” and “actively worked to displace Adam’s connections with family and loved ones.” OpenAI’s answer disputes what caused Adam’s death, cites the minor’s preexisting problems, emphasizes the extensive safety measures the company takes, and points out that “ChatGPT’s guardrails caused it to repeatedly refuse to respond to many of Adam Raine’s queries regarding self-harm” and “repeatedly (more than 100 times) directed him to reach out to loved ones, trusted persons, crisis hotlines and other crisis resources in connection with his queries about self-harm.”   

One reason the cases will keep coming is that numerous law firms are carving out practice areas for AI suicide lawsuits. As one firm puts it, “AI chatbots like Character.AI and ChatGPT allegedly engaged in predatory behavior and ignored suicide threats. If you or a loved one was harmed, contact us today.” The Tech Justice Law Project joined SMVLC in November in filing the seven lawsuits against OpenAI and Altman. Meetali Jain, the Project’s executive director, contends that “ChatGPT is a product designed by people to manipulate and distort reality, mimicking humans to gain trust and keep users engaged at whatever the cost.” States are also suing Character Technologies.

A Federal Trade Commission inquiry launched in September “to understand what steps, if any, companies have taken to evaluate the safety of their chatbots when acting as companions” and “to limit the products’ use by and potential negative effects on children” will keep pressure and press attention on conversational chatbots’ alleged dangers. The American Psychological Association’s backing of the FTC’s inquiry may fuel research into so-called “AI psychosis” that, if ever officially recognized as a psychiatric disorder, could bolster plaintiffs’ claims. OpenAI notes that “mental health conversations [on ChatGPT] that trigger safety concerns, like psychosis, mania, or suicidal thinking, are extremely rare. Because they are so uncommon, even small differences in how we measure them can have a significant impact on the numbers we report.”

Given these forces, the odds of more litigation are high. Makers of conversational chatbots should engage in numerous risk-mitigation measures, precisely as some companies already have. Attorney Andrew McArthur has explained that:

Companies utilizing AI chatbots, non-playable characters, virtual assistants, or other similar products or services should carefully review their quality assurance programs, safety standards, data collection practices, and intellectual property policies to consider whether they have adequate safeguards in place to mitigate potential harm and ensure compliance with legal and regulatory obligations.

Cases targeting conversational chatbots raise critical questions that settlements won’t resolve, including some issues also playing out in the social media addiction cases I’ve addressed. For example, First County Bank and Raine include causes of action based on strict product liability theories. There are fundamental disputes about whether chatbots constitute “products,” at least as that term is used within such theories, or whether they are “services” delivering speech to users. A second, case-by-case question involves causation of human behavior and harm because many factors influence suicides.

The ultimate issue may be whether the First Amendment protects speech generated by chatbots. If it does, that would likely thwart most tort causes of action, including negligence claims, unless plaintiffs can satisfy the Supreme Court’s demanding test for unlawful incitement to violence. I’ve argued that the First Amendment safeguards chatbot output, as have others. However, US District Judge Anne Conway ruled in Garcia that—at the early, motion-to-dismiss stage—she was “not prepared to hold that Character A.I.’s output is speech.”

The post Suicides, Settlements, and Unresolved Chatbot Issues: A Long Litigation Road Lies Ahead appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: Viewpoint Diversity Isn’t a Slogan. It’s the Point of a University.
item.dc:creator: Julia Cataneo
item.description:
A central problem in higher education today is not merely that universities are ideologically narrow. It is that many academic leaders have become unwilling even to name that narrowing as a problem.

The post Viewpoint Diversity Isn’t a Slogan. It’s the Point of a University. appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
The Stanford Daily recently hosted a revealing exchange about “viewpoint diversity.”

Jessica Riskin, a Stanford historian and leader of the campus chapter of the American Association of University Professors, argued that the term is not what it appears to be—that it has become a political slogan, an instrument of conservative interference in academic life.

Paul Brest, Stanford’s former law dean and a leader in the campus chapter of Heterodox Academy, offered a response: Viewpoint diversity is not a partisan cudgel. It is a basic condition of the university’s truth-seeking mission.

But the exchange reveals something deeper. A central problem in higher education today is not merely that universities are ideologically narrow. It is that many academic leaders have become unwilling even to name that narrowing as a problem.

Brest’s argument is straightforward and, in the classical liberal tradition, difficult to contest. Universities exist to test ideas, not to protect them. Intellectual progress depends on contestation. John Stuart Mill famously warned that an opinion held without challenge becomes a dead dogma rather than a living truth. A campus that lacks meaningful dissent does not become more enlightened. It becomes more fragile—more prone to groupthink, confirmation bias, and moral certainty masquerading as scholarship.

Brest also highlights an increasingly important point: Academic freedom is not threatened only by formal censorship. It is threatened by social conformity. The pressures that silence people today rarely arrive as explicit bans. They arrive as reputational risk, professional ostracism, and the tacit understanding—especially among junior faculty and students—that certain views are simply not speakable.

This is not a right-wing fantasy. The Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression‘s most recent National Speech Index found that nearly three-quarters of Americans believe free speech is headed in the wrong direction; a striking indicator of how widespread pessimism about open expression has become.

Riskin’s response is what makes the exchange so revealing. She does not deny the ideological homogeneity. She does not argue that heterodox perspectives flourish in faculty culture. Instead, she focuses on the genealogy of the phrase itself, linking “viewpoint diversity” to conservative activists and broader political efforts to reshape higher education.

This is a familiar institutional maneuver: When the substance of a critique is difficult to refute, attention shifts to the motives of those raising it.

Of course political actors invoke viewpoint diversity opportunistically. Political actors invoke every principle opportunistically. The question is whether the principle is true. And the fact that the academy increasingly treats the very language of intellectual pluralism as suspect suggests that something deeper is at stake.

Riskin also offers a telling straw man: If advocates were serious, she implies, they would demand more Marxists or other underrepresented left-wing perspectives. But the imbalance at elite universities is not that they lack radicals. Many departments are saturated with them. The missing viewpoints are often far more ordinary: Religious traditionalism, civic nationalism, heterodox liberalism, skepticism of identity essentialism, and the kinds of institutional conservatism held by millions of Americans outside the academy.

The problem is not that campuses lack exotic ideologies. It is that they increasingly lack normal ones.

This narrowing has real consequences. Riskin suggests that the excesses of DEI arise from administrators and consultants, not from a lack of viewpoint diversity. But that distinction collapses under scrutiny. Why did these bureaucracies become untouchable in the first place? Why did institutions outsource moral authority to ideological compliance offices? The answer is precisely that dissent became morally illegible.

Viewpoint diversity is not an aesthetic preference. It is how institutions resist capture. When disagreement is treated as harm, administrative power expands. When everyone thinks alike, bureaucracies metastasize. The DEI regime did not arise despite intellectual homogeneity. It arose because of it.

Higher education has increasingly redefined disagreement as danger and consensus as virtue. Academic freedom has quietly come to mean the freedom to affirm progressive norms, while dissent is managed through social sanction. The result is an academy that speaks endlessly about diversity while fearing diversity of thought.

Viewpoint diversity is not a conservative slogan or a Republican project. It is not a Trojan horse. It is the premise of liberal education. A university that cannot tolerate internal pluralism becomes something else: a credentialing system wrapped in moral performance, a guild enforcing consensus, a bureaucratic church without theology.

The Stanford exchange shows how far we have drifted. One side argues that disagreement is essential to truth. The other responds that the language of disagreement has been contaminated by politics. That is not an answer. It is an evasion.

If universities cannot defend viewpoint diversity as a neutral institutional good, then academic freedom has already collapsed—not by law, but by culture.

A university that fears disagreement cannot educate for freedom and it does not deserve the public’s trust.

The post Viewpoint Diversity Isn’t a Slogan. It’s the Point of a University. appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.

item.title: The AI Jobs Non-Apocalypse: An Update
item.dc:creator: Julia Cataneo
item.description:
Artificial intelligence is indeed affecting the US job market—as a powerful general-purpose technology should, at some point—but in ways that are narrower and slower than the apocalypse narrative suggests.

The post The AI Jobs Non-Apocalypse: An Update appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
item.content:encoded:
It’s been just over three years since ChatGPT launched—and maybe roughly three minutes since the last viral warning about an imminent white-collar “bloodbath.” Of course, as has been correctly said, it’s tough to make predictions, especially about the future.

So what does the data actually say so far? A growing stack of careful studies points in a remarkably consistent direction. Artificial intelligence is indeed affecting the US job market—as a powerful general-purpose technology should, at some point—but in ways that are narrower and slower than the apocalypse narrative suggests.

Start with timing. One recent paper finds that labor-market weakening in AI-exposed occupations began in early 2022—months before ChatGPT’s release. The authors argue this pattern points to broader macroeconomic and sectoral forces, including monetary tightening and a post-pandemic correction in tech hiring. That, rather than a clean break caused by generative AI alone. At the same time, AI-related skills such as writing, coding, and information synthesis remain valuable, with graduates exposed to them earning higher pay and finding jobs faster.

Another widely cited study, “Canaries in the Coal Mine” from the Stanford Digital Economy Lab, finds clearer AI-linked effects after careful statistical controls. The strongest signal emerges beginning around 2024 and is concentrated primarily among young workers in highly exposed occupations. Even there, the impact appears mainly to reflect reduced hiring rather than layoffs, with firms likely trimming junior inflows rather than displacing experienced staff.

Other analyses tell a similar story. According to the Dallas Fed, employment declines linked to AI exposure are small and concentrated among workers ages 20–24, driven mainly by reduced entry into those occupations rather than layoffs. The aggregate impact on overall unemployment remains minimal.

The big picture looks even less threatening. A recent analysis by The Economist notes that the US economy has added millions of white-collar jobs since late 2022. Real wages have risen. There has also been strong growth in technical, managerial, and coordination-heavy roles, including many often labeled AI “at risk.”

Put it all together and the state of play seems clear here in mid-February 2026. AI’s early job-market effects look like a shift at the entry level, at worst, not mass displacement. And one might reasonably conclude that hiring is slowing in routine knowledge roles even as demand rises for experienced, judgment-intensive, and AI-complementary work. At least the first phase of the Great AI Labor Transition appears evolutionary, not revolutionary—and certainly not catastrophic.

To quote the Stanford team, “We will be actively monitoring the labor market and updating our results periodically to see if the trends we identified persist, strengthen, reverse, or change in some other ways.” 

Same with me.

The post The AI Jobs Non-Apocalypse: An Update appeared first on American Enterprise Institute - AEI.
